title: Recycling Reconsidered: The Present Failure and Future Promise of Environmental Action in the United States By Samantha MacBride The MIT Press. 2011. 312 pages. $27 cloth
abstract: Recycling is one of the important accomplishments of the environmental movement. In the United States, cities and counties, both big and small, have enthusiastically embraced recycling as an important step in creating a greener society. Recycling has increasingly emerged as the best measure of civic engagement in the United States, attesting to its popularity as a solution to our collective environmental troubles. Yet the success of the movement offers an incomplete and misleading story of progress. In her book titled, Recycling Reconsidered: The Present and Future Promise of Environmental Action in the United States, Samantha MacBride draws our attention to the unfulfilled promises of recycling. Specifically, MacBride identifies three types of challenges to the successful adoption of recycling. The first, she argues, is the gradual co-optation and commodification of the recycling movement by firms seeking to prevent implementation of effective solid-waste management policies. Debated extensively within the Treadmill of Production school in environmental sociology, the commodification of the recycling program has led to consistent loss of jobs for communities through the implementation of labor-saving techniques, robotics and expert systems adopted by the state to manage billing, accounting and waste management (Gould 2004; Pellow 2007; Schnaiberg 1997). A second challenge for the recycling program, according to MacBride, is the fallacy of selective promotion of environmental values with greater attention devoted to individual consumer behavior and less to the national and increasingly global production and transfer of waste. A final challenge, she argues, rests on the ecological modernization view of environmental management that calls for the pursuit of green technologies as a solution to the dilemmas of capitalism. MacBride rightly suggests that the environmental movement's “simultaneous advocacy of these contradictory positions” has led to a “net growth in the material dimensions of the solid-waste problems that such solutions set out to address in the first place.”(5)
